coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots modified dendritic TiO2-CdS heterojunction for enhanced photodegradation of rhodamine and hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Dou, Shuangxin
Wang, Dongting
Shang, Qianqian
Kong, Xiangjin
Fang, Yuzhen
coredata.dc:description: Heterojunction modified by nanosized interfacial contact stands out in promoting the transport of photogenerated charge carriers. In this work, a dendritic ternary photocatalyst of CdS-TiO2 heterojunction modified by carbon dots (CDs) was prepared by simple hydrothermal method and impregnation method, and the catalysts were characterized by XRD, SEM, TEM, XPS, Uv-vis and ESI. It was found the ternary photocatalyst endows excellent performance in degradation and photolysis of water due to the features of high surface, rich surface function groups, and high conductivity of CDs. Compared with the CdS-TiO2 photocatalyst, the RhB degradation rate of CDs-CdS-TiO2 increased from 69.0 % to 84.5 % in 50 min, and the rate of hydrogen production increased by 3.0 times, reaching 790.6 μmol g−1 h−1. Dendritic CdS-TiO2 heterojunctions and decorated with CDs lead to the increase of the absorption range in the visible region. Combined with theoretical calculations, the introduce of CDs can improve the photo-generated electron-hole separation and carrier transfer rate in CdS-TiO2 heterojunction when electrons are transferred from TiO2 to CdS.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots modified dendritic TiO2-CdS heterojunction for enhanced photodegradation of rhodamine and hydrogen evolution

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heterojunction modified by nanosized interfacial contact stands out in promoting the transport of photogenerated charge carriers. In this work, a dendritic ternary photocatalyst of CdS-TiO2 heterojunction modified by carbon dots (CDs) was prepared by simple hydrothermal method and impregnation method, and the catalysts were characterized by XRD, SEM, TEM, XPS, Uv-vis and ESI. It was found the ternary photocatalyst endows excellent performance in degradation and photolysis of water due to the features of high surface, rich surface function groups, and high conductivity of CDs. Compared with the CdS-TiO2 photocatalyst, the RhB degradation rate of CDs-CdS-TiO2 increased from 69.0 % to 84.5 % in 50 min, and the rate of hydrogen production increased by 3.0 times, reaching 790.6 μmol g−1 h−1. Dendritic CdS-TiO2 heterojunctions and decorated with CDs lead to the increase of the absorption range in the visible region. Combined with theoretical calculations, the introduce of CDs can improve the photo-generated electron-hole separation and carrier transfer rate in CdS-TiO2 heterojunction when electrons are transferred from TiO2 to CdS.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: For the sake of ease up the global energy crisis and environmental pollution, the demand for photocatalyst is increasing every year [1–4]. The utilization of semiconductor photocatalysis technology has aroused an immense level of interest in recent times for the purpose of environmental purification, due to its exceptional capacity for harnessing solar energy and effectively treating pollutants [5–7]. However, the development of efficient photocatalyst still faces great challenges.

The photocatalytic effectiveness of TiO2 has been found to be constrained by two primary issues, namely its wide band-gap and low charge mobility, which resulted in reducing utilization of visible light, and leading to lower overall efficiency [8–10]. Meanwhile, metal chalcogenide nano-semiconductor photocatalyst has been widely studied because of excellent optical properties and appropriate band gap potential [11,12]. The cadmium sulphide (CdS) photocatalyst with visible light activity has narrow band-gap of 2.4 eV and low cost, which has been regarded as the most potential hydrogen evolution material in the field of photocatalytic materials. Nevertheless, its photocatalytic activity is not ideal due to its high photogenerated carrier recombination rate and serious photocorrosion [13–16].

In order to solve these inherent defects of CdS and TiO2, a lot of work has been done to improve the efficiency of photocatalyst [17–19], such as heterojunction [20,21], Z-scheme [22,23], cocatalyst [24,25], core-shell [26,27], formation of p-n junction [28,29] and surface modification [30,31]. Di and Deng's team utilized a two-step photo-deposition approach to deposit CoOx and MoS2 on CdS nanorods to serve as reduction/oxidation cocatalysts, which resulted in a significant increase in hydrogen production [32]. In addition, Gao's group manufactured Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst: {010} BiVO4/Ag/CdS with preferable migration rate of photo-generative carrier, which effectively improved the degradation of organic contaminants from water by photocatalyst [33]. According to the above research, the catalyst's capacity to absorb visible light can be significantly enhanced through the combination of CdS and TiO2. Additionally, the introduction of CdS would lead to a further improvement of the catalytic performance of TiO2.

The existence of cocatalyst accelerates the effect of charge separation in the field of optics. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CDs) with a size of no >10 nm combined with other photocatalysts have the ability to store photoelectrons, inhibit the recombination of photon-generated electron-hole, and promote near-infrared visible light absorption [34–38]. They have attracted wide-ranging attention owing to its outstanding applications in optics and electricity [39,40]. For example, the synthesis by vasudevan vinesh's team testified that CDs-modified boron-doped exfoliated g-C3N4 (CDs@B-ECN) photocatalyst has enlarged its light trapping capability. Besides, the interface interaction between CDs structure and B-ECN further boosted the separation of photogenerated carriers [41]. Furthermore, composite material CDs/UiO-66 MOG synthesized by this method was helpful for effective mineralization of organic matter and efficient degradation of pollutants, which was related to CDs as an electron acceptor promoting the generation of active free radicals [42].

Based on the above discussion, we exploited the pine-like structure of CdS-TiO2 with sol-gel method and simple hydrothermal method, and then prepared CDs -CdS-TiO2 catalyst by isometric impregnation of CdS-TiO2 in carbon point solution. After carbon dot modification, the catalytic performance of TiO2-CdS was improved for its proper band gap and high photogenerated carrier mobility. Such as, compared with CdS-TiO2, the RhB degradation rate of increased from 69.0 % to 84.5 % in1-CDs-CdS-TiO2 in 50 min, and the rate of hydrogen production increased 3.0 times in 2-CDs-CdS-TiO2. We also carried out DFT theoretical calculation to discuss the electronic structure and active reaction mechanism of the catalyst. The ternary composite CDs-CdS-TiO2 photocatalyst would offer novel avenues for achieving high-performance organic pollutant degradation, and effective photolysis of water and hydrogen evolution. As a result, it possesses immense potential for the creation of superior photocatalysts.

Tetrabutyl orthotitanate (C16H36O4Ti), anhydrous ethanol (CH3CH2OH), glacial acetic acid (CH3COOH), concentrated nitric acid (HNO3), thiourea (CH4N2S), cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate (Cd(NO3)2·4H2O), citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), rhodamine B (C28H31ClN2O3), deionized water (DI). All the chemical reagents are purchased from reagent factory of shanghai chemical, and they are analytical grade.

TiO2 is prepared by sol-gel method. Put the beaker filled with 25 mL of Tetrabutyl orthotitanate on a magnetic stirring apparatus to stir, then dilatorily add 40 mL of absolute ethanol into tetrabutyl orthotitanate, and tardily drop glacial acetic acid (inhibitor) with 4 mL and concentrated nitric acid with 1 mL to adjust the solution pH to 2–3. After 30 min of magnetic stirring and 30 min of ultrasonic oscillation, a uniformly dispersed solution is obtained, and the solution is marked as solution A; Fully mix approximately with deionized water (5 mL) and absolute ethanol (15 mL), and after magnetic stirring and ultrasonic oscillation for 30 min each, a well-proportioned scattered solution is obtained, which is marked as solution B. Gradually drop the liquid B into the liquid A, and mark it as the liquid C. After ultrasonic oscillation, a uniformly dispersed faint yellow solution is formed. Then, magnetic stirring and ultrasonic oscillation were performed again to form a uniformly distributed faint yellow solution for 30 min, respectively. According to actual situation, a proper amount of citric acid aqueous solution is added, agitated evenly, and aged in the air for a certain time to get yellow colloid. After that, the aged colloid was dried in the oven for 2 h at 100 °C, finally the yellow solid was produced.

And then the yellow solid was cooled down to the room temperature, and pulverized into small granules followed by calcination in muffle furnace at 500 °C for 2 h. Eventually, the sample was naturally cooled, and then milled again to acquire pure TiO2 catalyst.

Heterojunction of CdS-TiO2 was prepared using simple hydrothermal method. The composite mass ratio of CdS and TiO2 was 4:4 (1-CdS-TiO2), 3:4 (2-CdS-TiO2), 2:4 (3-CdS-TiO2) and 1:4 (4-CdS-TiO2), respectively, to prepare four samples. Take mCdS: mTiO2 = 4: 4 as an example to prepare products, 0.5328 g of CH4N2S, 2.1593 g of Cd (NO3)2·4H2O and 1.0 g of TiO2 were dispersed in deionized water with the volume of 50 mL. The suspension was kept at 180 °C for 4 h, and then the acquired product was centrifuged at 8500 r/min for 10 min after being cooled down. The centrifuged product was washed multiple times using absolute ethanol and deionized water, at last the washed sample was placed in the oven for 12 h at 60 °C to produce yellow solid CdS-TiO2, named 1-CdS-TiO2. Meanwhile, under the same experimental conditions, the other heterojunctions of CdS-TiO2 were synthesized by changing the addition amount of CH4N2S and Cd (NO3)2·4H2O.

Some modifications were made on the basis of the hydrothermal preparation of CDs reported in the literature. 3.15 g of citric acid and 1005 μL of ethylenediamine were added in 50 mL of deionized water. After citric acid solid particles were completely dissolved, the solution was heated in the oven to hydrothermal reaction for 5 h at 180 °C. Then the product was cooled down to room temperature naturally, a brown-black solution was obtained. After dialysis, a high-concentration solution of CDs can be obtained, which is transferred to a 500 mL brown volumetric flask and sealed for storage for later use.

CDs-CdS-TiO2 was synthesized by equal volume impregnation method. Through calculation, the water absorption rate of TiO2-CdS composite photocatalyst is 49.37 %, so the dosage of carbon dot solution required for the composite sample with the mass ratio of 4:4 is 0.4989 g, respectively. The carbon solution was added into the TiO2-CdS composite photocatalyst, which was moved into a self-sealing bag and shaken evenly. After soaking for a period of time at room temperature, samples were taken out, and finally dried for 4 hat 60 °C to get the product as 1-CDs-CdS-TiO2 ternary photocatalyst. In this case, the mass ratio of the three parts of the prepared ternary photocatalyst is 0.5:1:1. With the same experimental conditions as above, the ternary composite photocatalyst with mass ratio of 0.61:0.75:1 (2-CDs-CdS-TiO2), 0.59:0.5:1 (3-CDs-CdS-TiO2) and 0.47:0.25:1 (4-CDs-CdS-TiO2) was obtained, respectively, their preparation method is displayed clearly in Fig. 1 .

Powder X-ray diffraction(2θ = 2–80°; scanning speed: 5°/min) were collected by using Cu-Ka radiation (Smart Lab 9 KW, Rigaku, Japan; λ = 1.54178 Å) high power powder X-ray diffractometer. Using standard KBr pellet technology, Fourier transform infrared spectrawas gathered on Thermo Scientific NICOLET 6700 spectrometer (USA). Scanning electron microscopy was detected by Environmental SEM (FE-SEM, Multimode 8, America). Transmission electron microscopeimages were acquired by Talos F200X, FEI, America using 200 kV acceleration voltage. XPS (X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy) was performed using an ESCALAB 250 Xi spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Lambda 750 UV/VIS/NIR (PerkinElmer, America). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and transient photocurrent response and were tested by Electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai Chenhua, China) using three-electrode system. Furthermore, A 300 W Xe lamp, a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution, and conductive glass coated with 1*1cm2 sample were used as the light source, electrolyte, and working electrode, respectively.

The RhB aqueous solution photocatalytic degradation was carried out under 420 nm cut-off filter of 300 W Xe lamp. 0.1 g of catalyst was dispersed to RhB solution (60 mL, 10 mg/L) stirring for half an hour in dark. Subsequently, the light source is turned on, and for each group of experiments. For every sample test, 3 mL of suspension was filtered with the microporous filter (0.45 nm) for further analysis. The photodegradation efficiency of RhB was tested by spectrophotometer (UV-5100, Shanghai Yuan analysis Instrument Co., Ltd., China) at 554 nm of the characteristic absorption wavelength. The degradation efficiency η and degradation rate k of the catalyst are calculated as following:
ŋ =
C t

× 100 % ; k = ln

t

Photocatalytic performancewas evaluated as follow: the mixed aqueous solution of 10 mg photocatalyst and 4.43 vol% triethanolamine was transferred to a 100 mL sealed reactor. The light source was using xenon lamp of 300 W with the cut-off filter of λ ≥ 420 nm. The air in the reaction device were removed by vacuum pumping after photo-depositing platinum for one hour. The reaction solution temperature was controlled at 6 °C.The amount of hydrogen generated was analyzed by thermal conductivity detector in GC-7920 gas chromatograph (Beijing Zhongjiao Jinyuan Technology Co., Ltd., China).

Computational calculations have been performed by Vienna ab initio simulation package [43]. Geometry structure optimizations and electronic structures were calculated using exchange-correlation function on basis of generalized gradient approximation (GGA) of Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) using the cutoff of 400 eV [44,45]. The k-point determined in the light of Monkhorst-Pack scheme grid generated by KPT-Resolved value of 0.030 in the Brillouin zone. The ionic relaxation and electronic were set to −0.02 eV/Å and 10−8 eV, respectively.

The catalysts models of CdS, TiO2, CdS-TiO2 was constructed based on XRD and TEM results. To build slab models of CdS (100) and TiO2 (001), we first optimized the bulk CdS (8.413 Å × 4.2065 Å × 6.8429 Å) and anatase TiO2 (3.776 Å × 3.776 Å × 9.486 Å), and then used the layer periodic slab with 4 × 2 and 5 × 5 supercell both with the vacuum layers of 15 Å for CdS (100) and TiO2 (001). The heterojunction of CdS-TiO2 is composed by the optimized CdS (100) and TiO2 (001). Then molecules adsorption energies of H2 and RhB were calculated as the following:
Eads = Etotal −
Eslab + Eg
where Eads, Etotal, Eslab and Eg are the adsorption energies, energies of adsorption structures, the energy of the clean surfaces, and the adsorbed molecules in gas phase, respectively.

To explore the structure of all samples, we performed XRD characterization shown in Fig. 2 . In Fig. 2a, the prepared TiO2 characteristics of diffraction peaks were 25.3°, 37.8°, 48.0°, 53.9°, 55.1°, 62.9°, 68.8°, 70.3° and 75.1° corresponding to those lattice planes of (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116), (220) and (215) for tetragonal TiO2 (PDF # 21–1272), respectively. It is an obvious anatase characteristic peak around 25.28° [46,47]. The characteristic diffraction peaks of CdS-TiO2 binary heterojunction at 24.81°, 26.50° and 28.18° matching with the lattice planes of (100), (002) and (101) for hexagonal CdS (PDF # 41–1049), respectively. It means that CdS was successfully synthesized on TiO2 catalyst. At the same time, it is rather remarkable that the diffraction peak of (101) lattice plane of TiO2 is widened by the increasement of CdS. It is found out that the peak of CdS at 24.81° and that of TiO2 at 25.28° might overlap. As demonstrated in Fig. 2b, diffraction peak at 26.6° is ascribe to the (002) plance of hexagonal CDs (PDF # 126–1080). However, it was observed that the characteristic peak of CdS was evidently weakened after the introduction of CDs, which might be caused by the complexation of amine groups with CdS in solution and the shedding of CdS attached to TiO2. The diffraction peak of CDs-CdS-TiO2 showed similar to that of CdS-TiO2, which suggested the recombination of CDs and CdS did not alter the crystal structure of TiO2.

The material structure of the CDs-CdS-TiO2 photocatalyst was probed using Raman spectroscopy illustrated in Fig. 3 . As seen in Fig. 3a, the characteristic peaks at 144, 398, 515, and 637 cm−1 also demonstrated the presence of TiO2 anatase phase [48,49]. Additionally, Raman spectra revealed peaks at 300 cm−1 and 597 cm−1 were corresponding to 1LO and 2LO modes of CdS, respectively, which indicated that CdS materials existed in the synthesized catalyst [50]. These peaks, which signal CdS crystallinity and purity, become sharper with increasing CdS content. Focusing on the Raman curves in the range of 1000–1650 cm−1 in Fig. 3b, the intriguing peaks at 1344 cm−1 and 1578 cm−1 were attributed to the D band and G band of CDs, respectively [51]. Taken together, the characterization of Raman provided strong evidence of successful synthesis of the ternary composite photocatalysts of CDs-CdS-TiO2.

To verify the surface chemical valence states and chemical compositions of various elements, XPS characterization was performed. In Fig. 4a, XPS survey spectra demonstrated the existence of corresponding elements in CdS-TiO2, CDs-CdS-TiO2, and TiO2. In Fig. 4b, peaks of Ti 2p spectra were focused on 463.9–464.4 eV, and 458.1–458.8 eV, respectively, matching with Ti 2p1/2, and Ti 2p3/2. Compared with rutile TiO2, the Ti 2p peaks in 1-CdS-TiO2 sample exhibits a tiny shift to higher bonding energy, which indicates the interaction between CdS and anatase TiO2, the result is consistent with that of XRD. After the incorporation of CDs, the peak of Ti 2p still tends to move to higher bond energy, which may be due to the interaction between TiC, which changes the surface Ti chemical environment in the compound [52]. In Fig. 4c, two typical peaks of S 2p were detected at 162.2 eV and 161.1 eV, which respectively belong to the characteristic peaks of S2− 2p1/2 and S2− 2p3/2. Fig. 4d presents Cd 3d XPS spectrum, two distinct peaks of Cd are located at approximately 411.5 eV and 404.7 eV, attributing to Cd2+ 3d3/2 and Cd2+ 3d5/2 separately. For the XPS O1s spectrum of TiO2, as seen in Fig. 4e, the peak located at 530.0 eV is attributed to the existence of Ti-O-Ti. However, after the CDs is modified, the peak at 531.7 eV is discovered, which may be caused by the formation of Ti-OH bond [53]. As shown in the Fig. 4f, peaks centered at 288.2 eV, 284.6 eV and 285.9 eV were respectively allocated to O=C-O, C=O/C=N and CC groups, which testified the existence of CDs [54,55].

As exhibited in Fig. 4g, stretching vibration peaks of C-OH and NH at 3393 cm−1, and that of CH at 2924 cm−1, asymmetric stretching vibration of C-NH-C at 1242 cm−1, stretching vibration of CO at 1641 cm−1 and NH at 1582 cm−1 are given. The peak at1064 cm−1 is corresponds to CO vibration [56,57].

Electron microscope was used to explore the structure, size and microstructure of the photocatalyst. In Fig. 5a, TiO2 prepared by sol-gel method has a block structure with smooth surface. SEM images of CdS-TiO2 with different CdS contents were demonstrated in Fig. 5b and Fig. 5c, and it can be revealed that CdS gradually adheres to the surface of TiO2. As the increase of the composite ratio of CdS, microstructure of CdS-TiO2 gradually becomes pine-like, and the pine-like structure of 1-CdS-TiO2 is the most noticeable as seen in Fig. S1. Fig. 5d is the scanning electron microscope image of 1-CDs-CdS-TiO2. It can be observed that the dendritic structure of the sample tends to collapse, which may be due to the change of the structure caused by the heating reaction during the impregnation of CDs. As shown in Fig. S2 and Fig. 5e, we have prepared the uniform spherical particle morphology of CDs, and its dispersed particle size is about 3–4 nm. The lattice space of 0.21 nm is corresponding with pure CDs (CDs is highlighted in the red rectangle). In Fig. 5f, the high-resolution projection electron microscope of 1-CdS-TiO2 shows that it is a well pine branch structure, which corresponds to Fig. 5d. As exhibited in Fig. 5g, the inter planar crystal spacing of 0.33 nm is corresponding to (002) crystal plane of CdS, while the inter planar crystal spacing of 0.35 nm is corresponding to (101) crystal plane of TiO2. Element mapping of 1-CdS-TiO2 and 1-CDs-CdS-TiO2 is shown in Fig. 5f and Fig. 5g, which shows the equally distributed elements of C, Cd, S, Ti and O in samples. All results proved the CdS-TiO2 heterojunction formation, and CDs were successfully embellished onto the heterojunction of CdS-TiO2.

The light-harvesting performance of the catalyst was analyzed by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, and the absorption spectra of CdS-TiO2 and TiO2 are demonstrated in Fig. S3. As shown in Fig. S3, absorption edge of pure TiO2 was about 350 nm, while the absorption edge of CdS-TiO2 heterojunctions extended to about 480 nm, which leads to the increase of the absorption range in the visible region. As a result, the bandgap of CdS-TiO2 decreases due to the formation of heterojunction. Visible light absorption of the ternary photocatalyst after the introduction of CDs is shown in Fig. 6 .

As revealed Fig. 6a, it may be that the decorated with CDs increases the sensitivity of CdS-TiO2 catalyst to light, and its electrons have a transition in energy level, which improves the carrier, that is, changes the responsiveness of the catalyst to light. According to Tauc plot equation, the band gap energies is determined as shown below.
αhv
1 / n
= A
hv −
E g

In which, “h”,“α”, “v”, “A” and “Eg” were Planck constant, absorption coefficient, incident light frequency, the constant and band gap energy, respectively. “n” variables depend on the type band gap. As shown in Fig. 6b, the Eg of 1-CdS-TiO2, 2-CdS-TiO2, 3-CdS-TiO2 and 4-CdS-TiO2 are confirmed to be 2.26 eV, 2.24 eV, 2.23 eV and 2.27 eV, respectively. The Eg of 1-CDs-CdS-TiO2, 2-CDs-CdS-TiO2, 3-CDs-CdS-TiO2 and 4-CDs-CdS/TiO2 are 2.24 eV, 2.23 eV, 2.22 eV and 2.22 eV, respectively. With the introduction of carbon dots, it can be revealed the band gap width of CDs-CdS-TiO2 was smaller than that of CdS-TiO2, but it did not markedly affect the band gap width of ternary photocatalyst, which indicated that the incorporation of CDs might reduce the required energy for the electronic transition reaction of photocatalyst, and the photocatalytic reaction was easier to carry out.

We conducted photocurrent response test and EIS test to investigate the electrochemical properties of binary and ternary catalysts. Fig. 7a shows the transient photocurrent generated by the following ternary and binary catalysts of CdS-TiO2 with different mass ratios. The study found that 2-CdS-TiO2 and 2-CDs-CdS-TiO2 have the highest photocurrent density in CdS-TiO2 photocatalytic system and CDs-CdS-TiO2 photocatalytic system. The three-way catalyst doped with CDs has higher photocurrent density than the corresponding binary catalyst. Fig. 7b shows the EIS curve of photocatalyst. The smaller the half arc of the curve in electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, the stronger the ability of carrier transfer. It could be found when mass ratio of CdS to TiO2 is 3:4, the resistance arc radius of the catalyst was the smallest, that is, the electron mobility of CdS-TiO2 was the largest. The reason for this phenomenon may be that CdS and TiO2 form heterojunction structure, which changes the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons-holes and stimulates the charge transfer process. With the addition of CDs, the half arc of the impedance curve of the monolithic ternary photocatalyst is smaller than that of the binary photocatalyst, and the half arc of the impedance curve of 2-CDs-CdS-TiO2 was the smallest. The transient photocurrent test and electrochemical impedance test both proved that the introduction of CDs can encourage the generation of photo-generated carriers validly and increased the separation efficiency of the photo-generated electron-hole pairs.

The prepared products were tested by RhB photodegradation under simulated sunlight. Fig. 8a exhibits the efficiency of photocatalytic RhB degradation at different irradiation time. The behavior of direct RhB photolysis was compared as blank experiment. For TiO2, the RhB degradation increased with the prolongation of light time, 22 % of RhB was degraded after 50 min of light irradiation. Secondly, different proportions of CdS were introduced into TiO2 samples, and the binary composite samples obtained displayed high photocatalytic activity. It can be witnessed that the RhB photodegradation of 1-CdS-TiO2 can reach 59.8 % within 50 min of illumination.

The RhB degradation kinetics was studied by first-order kinetic model: ln
C 0
C t
= kt , In this formula, C0, Ct and t represent the initial concentration, the concentration at t min, and the irradiation time, respectively. RhB concentration and reaction rate constant during irradiation, as seen in Fig. 8b, fitting results indicated that the k values of TiO2, 1-CdS-TiO2, 2-CdS-TiO2, 3-CdS-TiO2 and 4-CdS-TiO2 were 0.00416, 0.0178, 0.01545, 0.0042 and 0.0034 min−1, respectively. The k value of 1-CdS-TiO2 was significantly higher than that of other samples.

Then, the photocatalytic performance of ternary heterojunction formed by carbon dots and CdS-TiO2 was also evaluated by the degradation of RhB. As displayed in Fig. 8c, the activities of CDs-CdS-TiO2 ternary photocatalysts were significantly higher than those of CdS-TiO2 binary photocatalyst. The degradation efficiency of 1-CDs-CdS-TiO2, 2-CDs-CdS-TiO2, 3-CDs-CdS-TiO2 and 4-CDs-CdS-TiO2 was 84.5 %, 66.7 %,52.1 % and 56.9 % respectively. It can be calculated that the degradation efficiency of 1-CDs-CdS-TiO2 photocatalyst was 62.5 % and 24.7 % higher than those of TiO2 and 1-CdS-TiO2 respectively. The degradation kinetics of RhB was analyzed to determine the reaction rates of various ternary photocatalysts. On the basis of the fitting result in Fig. 8d, the k values of 1-CDs-CdS-TiO2, 2-CDs-CdS-TiO2, 3-CDs-CdS-TiO2 and 4-CDs-CdS-TiO2were 0.03435, 0.02223, 0.01549 and 0.01697 min−1, respectively. As a result, the k value of 1-CDs-CdS-TiO2 was 8.25 and 1.92 times higher than those of TiO2 and 1-CdS-TiO2. This means that the introduction of CdS and CDs can promote the photocatalytic activities significantly.

Using triethanolamine as sacrificial agent, the photocatalytic hydrogen evolutions for the above catalysts was investigated under visible light. According to Fig. 8e, the production of hydrogen rised as the radiation time lengthens for all specimens. It was intuitively proved that the ternary photocatalyst CDs-CdS-TiO2 was more beneficial to photocatalytic hydrogen production than the binary photocatalyst CdS-TiO2. Despite the low hydrogen production of the CdS-TiO2 sample, the incorporation of carbon dots into the photocatalyst compound enhanced its capacity to produce hydrogen. Among them, the hydrogen production of 2-CDs-CdS-TiO2 was the highest, which was 3162.2 μmol g−1, which was 3.0 times of the hydrogen production of 1-CdS-TiO2. Meanwhile, from the hydrogen production rate chart in Fig. 8f, it can also be found that the hydrogen production rate of photocatalyst introduced into carbon dots has been significantly upgraded. 2-CDs-CdS-TiO2 had the highest hydrogen production rate of 790.6 μmol g−1 h−1. It was possible the introduction of CDs increased active sites of photocatalyst and accelerated the charge transfer. The aforementioned results collectively imply that the implementation of heterojunction structures, exemplified through CDs-CdS-TiO2, yields a highly desirable photocatalytic activity for both hydrogen production and degradation. Therefore, it holds tremendous potential for an extensive range of applications in the field of photocatalysis.

The stability of a photocatalyst is of paramount importance in industrial applications. In this study, we recovered the CDs-CdS-TiO2 ternary catalyst after the hydrogen evolution reaction and examined its structural integrity using XRD analysis, which is presented in Fig. S4. Comparison of the CDs-CdS-TiO2 photocatalyst before and after the four-hour reaction indicated that the characteristic peaks of the photocatalyst had slightly weakened, but no significant changes were observed in the main characteristic peaks. As illustrated in Fig. 9a, upon conducting four photocatalytic cyclic hydrogen evolution tests on the 2-CDs-CdS-TiO2 photocatalyst that exhibited the highest hydrogen production performance, a marginal decline in hydrogen production was observed after the fourth cycle, constituting 80.3 % of its initial value. In the initial stages of the reaction, the catalyst displayed an augmented hydrogen production rate that subsequently stabilized in later stages, which could be linked to the photo-corrosion of CdS. To gauge the structural stability of the photocatalyst post-recycling, XRD and XPS patterns of 2-CDs-CdS-TiO2 before and after 16 h of cyclic reaction were procured, as exemplified in Fig. 9b and Fig. 9c. Our analysis revealed that the characteristic peak of the crystal structure of photocatalyst remained in the original position before and after recycling, which indicated that it had high reusability and stability [58–60].

On the basis of catalyst characterizations of XRD and HRTEM, models of photocatalyst were selected as exhibited in Fig. S5, and the calculated XRD of TiO2 and CdS models in Fig. S6 agreed with the experimental results in Fig. 2, which indicates that our theoretical models were reasonable. The optimized configurations and partial bond length parameters are shown in Fig. 10 . In the pre-optimized configuration in Fig. 10b, the interfaces of CdS (100) and TiO2 (001) were constructed into heterojunctions, and their distance between interfaces was 3 Å, the distances of STi and CdO were 3.430 Å and 3.961 Å. After structure relaxation, the interactions of heterostructure can enhance by the new formed chemical bonds of STi and CdO with the bond lengths values of dS-Ti = 2.594 Å and dCd-O = 2.379 Å as shown in Fig. 10a. When CdS-TiO2 heterojunction is modified with CDs in Fig. 10c, the bond lengths of dS-Ti and dCd-O further decrease to 2.427 Å and 2.261 Å, and part of the Cd atoms will also migrate towards CDs under the action of hydrogen bonds in CDs. These changes between structures of CdS-TiO2 and CDs-CdS-TiO2 are mainly due to interactions between interfaces after the formation of heterojunction.

To gain further mechanistic insights into the interaction between interfaces, work function, density of states, partial density of states of Ti, O, Cd and S, bader charge and charge density are calculated demonstrated in Fig. 11 , Fig. S7, Table 1 and Fig. S8.

Work Function (Φ) can be used to judge the charge transfer at the interface of heterojunction.As shown in Fig. 11a and b, Φ values of pure CdS and pure TiO2 surfaces were 5.5966 and 5.4268 eV calculated by the formula of Φ = Evac-Efermi, respectively. Whereas, Evac and Efermi are vacuum level and fermi energy. Electrons will flow from TiO2 to CdS when the TiO2 contacts with the surface of CdS as the work Function of TiO2 is lower than that of CdS. This result is in accord with the characterization results of XPS. According to the XPS characterization results, after the formation of heterojunctions, the peaks of Ti 2p shifts to a high angle and the electron cloud decreases, which also indicates the direction of electrons transfer from TiO2 to CdS between heterojunctions. The density of states of CdS-TiO2 in Fig. 11c and Fig. S7 indicate the conduction-band minimum is composed of hybrid orbitals of Ti 3d and Cd 4f, meanwhile, the valence-band maximum of CdS-TiO2 is made up of hybrid orbitals of O 2pand S 3p mainly. The photoinduced electrons may originate from the O 2p and S 3p orbital to the hybrid orbitals of Ti 3d and Cd 4f concluded by the DOS result. By calculating average bader charge in Table 1 and charge density in Fig. S8, it was found that the highest charge density is located at Cd and Ti atoms. The number of outer electrons of O and S increased after the formation of CdS-TiO2 heterojunctions.

The charge density differences of RhB adsorbed on CdS (100), TiO2 (001), CdS-TiO2and CDs-CdS-TiO2weredisplayed in Fig. 12 . As shown in Fig. 12, electron transfer between catalyst surface and reactant was more obvious, photocatalystsof CdS, TiO2, CdS-TiO2 and CDs-CdS-TiO2 all have different activation degree to the RhB reactant molecules for their adsorption energies of −2.4406 eV, −6.8409 eV, −0.9934 eV and − 1.4903 eV, respectively. TiO2 has the strongest adsorption capacity of RhB, however, its catalytic activity was the worst as shown in the catalytic performance test. The main reason was that the adsorption of Cl atom in RhB on the catalyst interface of TiO2 was strongest, which can be obtained from the atomic charge coloring diagram demonstrated in Fig. S8, Ti (red) and O (blue) in TiO2 had distinct positive and negative charges, and its degradation products would also be the least susceptible to desorption.

Based on the charge density difference for the different catalysts shown in Fig. 12, it is obvious that Cl atom in RhB grasp rich electrons (cyan color region), and the strong chemical bonds of ClTi and ClCd are formed on the surface of pure TiO2 and CdS. As CdS and TiO2 formed heterojunction, there was strong interaction between them, which weakened the adsorption activation of RhB with very little electron transfer from CdS-TiO2 to RhB. The introduction of CDs is conducive to regulating the adsorption strength of RhB on the catalyst, and the detailed path of electron transmission is shown below: electrons in O 2p and S 3p orbital of CdS-TiO2 are firstly transit to hybrid orbitals of Ti 3d and Cd 4f. Then the photogenerated electrons can excited to C 2s orbital of CDs and finally participate in the next photo-catalytic reactions. CDs promote the migration of photocarriers in CdS-TiO2, which may be the major factors for catalytic performance of photocatalytic degradation of RhB with the order of CDs-CdS-TiO2 > CdS-TiO2 > TiO2.

The free energy (△GH⁎) of H adsorption results were displayed in Fig. 13 , which is obtained by the formula [61] of △GH⁎ = △EH + △EZPE-T△SH. Whereas, △EH, △EZPE, T, and △SH represent adsorption energy of hydrogen, zero-point energy difference, thermodynamic temperature, and Entropy change, respectively. △GH⁎was one of the key parameters to evaluate the performance of hydrogen evolution reaction,

The △GH⁎ values were − 1.5339 eV, 2.7826 eV,1.4402 eV and -1.2084 eV for the catalysts of CdS, TiO2, CdS-TiO2 and CDs-CdS-TiO2, respectively, which means H⁎ would occupy the main active sites on CdS surface to suppress subsequent H⁎ to adsorb on the catalyst continuously. △GH⁎ of TiO2 is high with the value of 2.7826 eV, which means hydrogen atomsare unstable on the surface, and are hard to transfer proton. As a result, pure CdS and TiO2 would have an un-satisfactory performance of HER. However, CdS-TiO2 and CDs-CdS-TiO2 for its more suitable free energy than the single catalyst of CdS or TiO2 would be ideal catalyst for hydrogen evolution, especially for the catalyst of CDs-CdS-TiO2, which is also agree with the test results of hydrogen evolution of catalysts.

To reveal the photocatalytic mechanism, (αhν)2 vs hν curves and the valence band-XPS spectra of CdS and TiO2 were illustrated in Fig. S9a, Fig. S9b and S9c. From the (αhν)2 vs hν curves of TiO2 and CdS in Fig. S9a, it can be inferred that the Eg values of TiO2 and CdS are 3.11 eV and 2.30 eV, respectively. By means of scrutiny and analysis of band-XPS spectra, it can be discerned that the positions where the valence bands of CdS and TiO2 manifest are 1.48 eV and 2.64 eV, respectively. By utilizing the formulas EVB = φ + EVB, xps - 4.5 and EVB = Eg + ECB (where EVB denotes valence bands; ECB represents conduction band; Φ (4.47 eV) is the work function of XPS instrument), EVB,TiO2, EVB,CdS, ECB,TiO2 and EVB,CdS can be calculated, and they are 2.61 eV, 1.45 eV, −0.5 eV and − 0.85 eV, respectively [62–65]. By merging the valence and conduction band positions of CdS and TiO2 with the outcomes computed from the work function, a likely photocatalytic mechanism for the production of hydrogen and degradation utilizing the CDs-CdS-TiO2 heterojunction was put forward, as depicted in Fig. 14 . By calculating the work function, it is noted that CDs-CdS-TiO2 catalyst may belong to Z-scheme heterojunction, in which electrons are transferred from TiO2 to CdS. Photogenerated electrons in TiO2 conduction band will recombine with holes in CdS valence band leading to the accumulation of electrons in CdS valence band [66]. At the same time, CDs, a cocatalyst with electron trapping ability contributes to prevent photogenerated electrons from recombination with holes, thus improving the hydrogen production rate of the catalyst; Under the irradiation of light, the excess holes in VB of TiO2 can participate in the oxidation reaction of RhB, which makes the catalyst have excellent degradation activity.

In summary, CDs-modified dendritic CdS-TiO2 was synthesized by simple impregnation method. Photoelectron properties, morphology changes and structural characterizations have been performed. The introduction of CDs could enhance the amount of charge extraction and photon capture capability at the CDs-CdS-TiO2 interface. About 84.5 % of RhB was effectively photodegraded by 1-CDs-CdS-TiO2 under visible light irradiation for 50 min. Hydrogen production of three-component photocatalyst after the introduction of CDs was remarkably elevated, and the production of hydrogen for 2-CDs-CdS-TiO2 was as high as 3162.2 μmol g−1. The photo-catalytic performance of CdS-TiO2was significantly improved by the modification of CDs in RhB degradation and in water splitting. Through theoretical calculations, we have discovered that the photocatalyst may possess a Z-scheme heterojunction structure, in which electrons are transferred from TiO2 to CdS. The modification of CDs can strengthen the interaction between the TiO2 and CdS interface, thus serving as the fundamental rationale for the superior catalytic performance of the CdS-CdS-TiO2 catalyst. The present study offers a novel approach towards ternary heterojunction photocatalysts.

Shuangxin Dou: Data collection, Experiment and writing mansucript draft; Dongting Wang & Qianqian Shang: Analyzing and revising; Xiangjin Kong: Conceptualization, funding support, editing. Yuzhen Fang: Conceptualization, calculation, data interpretation, review and editing.
